data.title: Transocean wins $158M Eastern Mediterranean five-well contract, adds to ~$1.6B April backlog
data.description:
Transocean announced a five-well, ~390-day Deepwater Asgard contract worth approximately $158 million in backlog starting Q4 2026.

Key Highlights:

  • Deepwater Asgard awarded a five-well Eastern Mediterranean campaign estimated at 390 days.
  • Contract expected to add approximately $158 million in backlog, excluding extra services and mobilization/demobilization pay.
  • Work is slated to commence in the fourth quarter of 2026, extending utilization of the drillship.
  • Including recent fixtures in Norway and Brazil, total backlog additions approximate $1.6 billion since early April.

Original SEC Filing: Transocean Ltd. [ RIG ] - 8-K - Apr. 16, 2026

data.title: Key reports Q1 2026 net income $486M, revenue $1.95B; EPS $0.44
data.description:
Key reported first-quarter 2026 net income from continuing operations attributable to common shareholders of $486 million, or $0.44 per diluted common share, up 31% year-over-year. Total taxable-equivalent revenue was $1.95 billion, an increase of 10% year-over-year, with net interest income (TE) of $1.23 billion and noninterest income of $723 million. The bank repurchased $389 million of common shares during the quarter and maintained an estimated Common Equity Tier 1 ratio of 11.4% at March 31, 2026.

Financial Highlights

  • Total revenue (taxable-equivalent): $1.95 billion for Q1 2026, up 10.2% vs. Q1 2025.
  • Net interest income (taxable-equivalent): $1.23 billion for Q1 2026, up 11.3% vs. Q1 2025; net interest margin (TE) 2.87% (up 29 bps YoY).
  • Noninterest income: $723 million for Q1 2026, up $55 million vs. Q1 2025.
  • Net income from continuing operations attributable to Key common shareholders: $486 million; diluted earnings per share from continuing operations: $0.44 (Q1 2026).
  • Capital and shareholder returns: repurchased $389 million of common shares in Q1 2026; estimated Common Equity Tier 1 ratio 11.4% at March 31, 2026.

Business Highlights

  • Loan growth skewed to commercial: period-end loans increased $2.6 billion sequentially; average commercial and industrial loans rose materially year-over-year and quarter-over-quarter, supporting higher net interest income.
  • Commercial Bank momentum: Commercial Bank reported higher net income of $451 million for Q1 2026 (up 13% YoY) driven by loan growth and fee businesses such as investment banking and service charges.
  • Fee-based businesses performing: priority fee businesses — investment banking, commercial payments, and wealth management — collectively grew 12% year-over-year, contributing to noninterest income expansion.
  • Balance sheet and deposit mix management: average deposits were $147.3 billion with intentional runoff of brokered time deposits and lower deposit costs, contributing to reduced deposit expense and improved margins.
  • Asset quality remained stable: nonperforming assets were 0.63% of period-end portfolio loans and OREO, and net charge-offs were 0.38% of average total loans for the quarter.

Original SEC Filing: KEYCORP /NEW/ [ KEY ] - 8-K - Apr. 16, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: Insteel Industries reports Q2 revenue $172.7M, net earnings $5.2M; gross margin 9.6%
data.description:
Insteel Industries reported second-quarter fiscal 2026 net earnings of $5.2 million, or $0.27 per diluted share, on net sales of $172.7 million for the quarter ended March 28, 2026. Gross profit for the quarter was $16.5 million, representing a 9.6% gross margin, and the company ended the quarter with $15.1 million in cash and no outstanding debt on its revolving credit facility. Management cited weather-driven shipment disruptions and narrower selling-price to raw-material-cost spreads as primary drivers of the quarter's results, while remaining optimistic about underlying demand.

Financial Highlights

  • Revenue: Net sales of $172.7 million for the three months ended March 28, 2026 (up from $160.7 million prior-year quarter).
  • Gross profit: $16.5 million for the quarter; gross margin of 9.6% (compared with 15.3% in prior-year quarter).
  • Operating results: Selling, general and administrative expense of $9.7 million for the quarter; other operating items include interest expense of $23, and interest income of $61.
  • Net income: Net earnings of $5.2 million, or $0.27 diluted EPS, for the quarter (compared with $10.2 million, $0.52 diluted EPS, in prior-year quarter).
  • Liquidity and cash flow: Ended the quarter with $15.1 million in cash and no borrowings outstanding on a $100.0 million revolver; operating activities provided $4.8 million of cash in the quarter.

Business Highlights

  • Shipment trends: Shipments declined 5.9% year-over-year for the quarter, driven mainly by widespread and prolonged winter weather disruptions across most markets that limited construction activity and operating schedules.
  • Pricing actions: Average selling prices rose 14.2% year-over-year for the quarter (1.0% sequentially), reflecting pricing initiatives to offset higher raw material and operating costs.
  • Operational efficiency: Elevated unit manufacturing costs and weather-related operational inefficiencies materially affected margins during the quarter.
  • Market outlook and demand: Management described delayed (not cancelled) project deliveries due to weather, expressed confidence in healthy underlying demand—particularly in nonresidential construction—and anticipates a seasonal pickup and recovery of weather-delayed shipments.
  • Strategic focus: Continued emphasis on disciplined pricing, operational efficiency, cost and productivity improvement initiatives, and growth investments in engineered structural mesh (ESM) business; capital expenditures expected up to approximately $20.0 million for fiscal 2026.

Original SEC Filing: INSTEEL INDUSTRIES INC [ IIIN ] - 8-K - Apr. 16, 2026

Disclaimer

data.title: Top officials have offered Putin ideas for economic growth after contraction, Kremlin says
data.description:
Russia's top economic officials have made proposals to President Vladimir Putin about how to kick-start the economy after he scolded them for a contraction in the first two months of the year, the Kremlin said on Thursday.

Russia's war economy, which contracted in 2022 but grew in 2023, 2024 and 2025, has outperformed the G7 average since 2023, though it slowed down last year and Putin said on Wednesday that the economy had contracted in January and February .

At the start of a Kremlin meeting with Russia's most powerful economic officials on Wednesday, Putin said the trajectory of the economy was below expectations and asked officials to give him detailed options on what could be done to remedy the situation.

Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said the closed part of Wednesday's meeting went on for several hours behind closed doors and that there had been a free exchange of opinions.

"The members of the government's economic bloc have many proposals to activate the economy and give it greater momentum," Peskov told reporters.

He declined to outline any details of the proposals for the economy, which has likely benefited from soaring prices for oil and gas triggered by the U.S. and Israeli attack on Iran.

data.title: France's antitrust watchdog fines organic food wholesalers for price fixing
data.description:
France's antitrust watchdog fined four organic food wholesalers, including one owned by Carrefour , a combined 12.7 million euros ($14.96 million) for colluding to fix their product prices over a seven-year period up to 2024.

The companies set up a "unique, complex and continuous agreement" to separate organic food product brands sold in specialized organic stores and regular supermarkets to prevent price comparison, which would have led to a general price decrease in organic stores, Autorite de la Concurrence said in a statement on Thursday.

The collusion happened as sales of organic food products expanded in France, mainly in supermarkets.

Synadis Bio was fined 10 million euros, while Carrefour's Greenweez was fined 1.85 million euros, ITM Enterprises, which operates Intermarche supermarkets, was ordered to pay 740,000 euros, and Les Comptoirs de la Bio, 80,000 euros.

The four companies fined did not immediately respond to requests for comment. They can appeal the decision in court.

The agreement started in 2017, the statement said, quoting board meeting transcripts from Synadis Bio in reaction to the sale of products sold by Bio C Bon supermarkets on internet retail giant Amazon .

"This represents a new and worrying competing activity for specialized organic stores because it blurs the lines between distribution channels and promotes price comparability," Synadis Bio's board said at the time, according to the watchdog.

Synadis Bio proceeded into sealing agreements with different wholesalers to keep different brands in the different kinds of stores, the watchdog said.

"The Authority considers this practice to be serious insofar as it has undeniably limited intra-brand and inter-brand competition, as well as price competition, between the two distribution channels," the statement said.

Overall sales of organic food products quadrupled between 2010 and 2020, while specialized organic stores' market share fell by 5 percentage points.

($1 = 0.8488 euros)

data.title: Middle East Crude-Benchmarks edge lower amid U.S.-Iran peace talk hope
data.description:
Middle East crude benchmark for Oman, Dubai and Murban premiums edged lower on Thursday, with hopes that easing U.S.-Iran tensions will soon lift global supplies.

Optimism grew on Thursday that the Iran war may be near an end, with a key Pakistani mediator having made a breakthrough on "sticky issues", a source said, although Iran warned the fate of its nuclear program had not been resolved.

The United States and Pakistan have been talking up the prospects for a deal in the more than six-week war, with U.S. President Donald Trump saying the accord would open the crucial Strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of the world's oil and gas supply flows.

Closure of the strait has triggered the worst oil price shock in history and forced the International Monetary Fund to downgrade its outlook for the global economy, warning prolonged conflict could push the world to the brink of recession.

SINGAPORE CASH DEALS

Cash Dubai's premium to swaps fell 33 cents to $12.49 a barrel.

Norwegian oil and gas company Equinor's downstream division, which includes energy trading, will report stronger first-quarter results this year, partly driven by volatility from the war in the Middle East, the group said on Thursday.

Spanish energy group Repsol has reached an agreement with the Venezuelan government and state energy firm PDVSA to take back operational control of its Venezuelan oil assets and boost production, the company said on Thursday.

data.title: BNY Profit Rises on Fee, Net Interest Income Growth
data.description:
By Nicholas G. Miller

Bank of New York Mellon reported higher first-quarter profit, boosted by growth in fee revenue and net interest income.

The company posted net income of $1.63 billion, or $2.24 a share, up from $1.22 billion, or $1.58 a share, the year prior.

Adjusted earnings were $2.25 a share. Analysts polled by FactSet had expected $1.93 a share.

Revenue rose 13% to $5.41 billion. Wall Street had expected $5.18 billion.

Fee revenue increased 11%, due to higher client activity and new business, higher market values and foreign exchange revenue and a favorable impact of a weaker U.S. dollar.

Net interest income grew 18%, reflecting the continued reinvestment of investment securities at higher yields and balance sheet growth.

Write to Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com

data.title: The 10-Point: The Wall Street Journal's Guide to the Day's Top News
data.description:
By Emma Tucker

This morning, we bring you new reporting on the Molotov cocktail-style attack on OpenAI CEO Sam Altman's home: Months before his arrest for allegedly attempting to murder Altman, Daniel Moreno-Gama suggested "Luigi'ing some tech CEOs" in an internet chat. The Texas college student's casual reference to Luigi Mangione — the accused UnitedHealthcare CEO killer — came during an online conversation with podcast producers, according to screenshots shared with the Journal. Moreno-Gama has yet to enter a plea. The incident spotlights a brewing anticorporate fervor in some internet subcultures, amplified by the national attention on Mangione, who has pleaded not guilty.

Today's Headlines

The Trump administration wants automakers and other American manufacturers to play a larger role in weapons production, reminiscent of a practice used during World War II.

Iran's war-shattered economy means it has an urgent reason to negotiate. Follow our live coverage of the conflict here.

California Rep. Eric Swalwell's stunning downfall creates a reckoning for Democrats.

Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni's quest to hold the U.S.-Europe alliance together is crumbling.

New York Gov. Kathy Hochul's proposed pied-Ã -terre tax on second homes in NYC valued at $5 million or more is gaining momentum.

Live From The Markets

Spirit Airlines is facing a steeper climb out of bankruptcy due to rising fuel prices.

Washington handed big banks a windfall — but they aren't spending it on Main Street, writes Telis Demos.

Read It Here First

A private equity billionaire mounts his biggest takeover yet: the Pentagon.

On Wall Street, Steve Feinberg had a well-oiled sales pitch for investors thinking of betting billions on his corporate turnarounds. Now the Pentagon's No. 2 official, the former private-equity boss faces the biggest sell of his career: persuading Congress to bless the Trump administration's $1.5 trillion military budget. The massive funding surge would pour into a military plagued by years of costly overruns and painful delays. Feinberg has told Trump administration officials he could avoid those pitfalls by using a carrot-and-stick approach with companies on the receiving end of those dollars, report Drew FitzGerald, Lara Seligman and Marcus Weisgerber.

There's a decades-old legal question at the heart of the Fed chair showdown.

President Trump's threat to fire Jerome Powell has led Washington into uncharted legal territory: When the Federal Reserve chair's term expires without a confirmed successor, who gets to decide what happens next? The law doesn't clearly say — and the White House and Fed have very different answers, reports Nick Timiraos.

Expert Take

Q: Has the era of the mega-layoff arrived?

From Snap to Block to Amazon, a new template for "right-sizing" the workforce is spreading through C-suites — and other companies are taking notes, reports WSJ's Chip Cutter.

A: Employers such as Oracle and Amazon are seizing on the potential financial upsides of severing swaths of their workforces at once.

Not long ago, mass layoffs registered as a sign of trouble or mismanagement but now, a company is more likely to get a big stock bump and praise from investors for acting boldly.

Block shares had fallen 16% this year before it laid off nearly half of its employees in late February. The stock has since reversed those losses, and then some.

The big cuts reflect a fundamental shift in how U.S. companies view their professional talent. Corporate leaders have come to see large teams as impeding progress. The rationale appears driven less by AI's ability to replace workers outright than by the soaring costs of building the technology, executives say.

Regardless of the why, executives say, companies are finding a way to slash jobs and are being rewarded by investors for it.

Scammers are stealing recruiters' identities to target job seekers.

See The Story

Vance walks a political tightrope in the Trump-Pope feud.

JD Vance is under strain from all sides. WSJ's Washington coverage chief Damian Paletta explains how pressure from Trump, the Iran talks, Pope Leo and the conservative right are shaping the vice president's role.

Happening Today

U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Gen. Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, are set to hold a press briefing at 8 a.m. ET.

Economic data: The Labor Department reports weekly jobless claims.

Earnings: Netflix, PepsiCo, U.S. Bancorp, Abbott Laboratories, Charles Schwab

Number Of The Day:

$3,642

The average refund amount this tax season, up $346 or 11.1% from the same time last year. Americans are getting bigger refunds or owing less this tax season. Driving the gains is a Republican tax law set to dole out over $100 billion in relief this year. New tax deductions for overtime, tips and seniors — plus a bigger deduction for state and local taxes — are padding wallets.

And Finally...

The bar made famous by 'Out of Africa' still bans women. They've had enough.

Nairobi's venerable Muthaiga Country Club, opened in 1913 for the British elite, has been embroiled in a heated debate over whether it should allow women in its Member's Bar. The bar is best known for turning away Meryl Streep's character in the 1985 film.

data.title: BNY's profit rises on strong fees, interest income growth​
data.description:
BNY reported a rise in first-quarter profit on Thursday, as the world's largest custodian bank was helped by strong fee income and higher values of client assets.

Global markets have swung sharply due to the Iran war and a broad selloff in AI-threatened software stocks, prompting investors and investment managers to rebalance portfolios, and boosting client activity.

BNY, which makes a sizeable chunk of its income safeguarding and servicing client assets, saw assets under custody and administration climb 12% year-on-year to $59.4 trillion as of March 31, while assets under management stood at $2.1 trillion.

Fee revenue, the bank's largest source of income, jumped 11% in the quarter to $3.77 billion, supported by higher market levels and continued client engagement.

BNY's net interest income - the spread between earnings from assets and costs on liabilities - surged 18% to $1.37 billion, boosted by higher yields on the reinvestment of matured assets.

CEO Robin Vince has been pushing to streamline operations and invest in technology to improve efficiency and drive growth. Return on tangible common equity - a metric that tracks profitability using only hard assets - rose to 29.3% in the quarter from 24.2% a year ago.

"When there are activity levels, when there is quantity issuance, and there's a lot of fixed income issuance that's been going on this year for all the reasons that you're well aware of, our business is firing on many of these cylinders," Vince told Reuters in an interview.

The company posted net income of $1.63 billion, or $2.24 per share, compared with $1.22 billion, or $1.58 per share, a year earlier. Revenue rose to a record $5.4 billion, up 13% from a year ago.

​

data.title: Transocean Ltd. Files 8K - Regulation FD >RIG
data.description:
Transocean Ltd. (RIG) filed a Form 8K - Regulation FD Disclosure - with the U.S Securities and Exchange Commission on April 16, 2026.

Transocean Ltd. ("Transocean") today announced that the Deepwater Asgard was awarded a five-well contract in the Eastern Mediterranean Sea with an undisclosed operator. The estimated 390-day campaign is expected to commence in the fourth quarter of 2026 and contribute approximately $158 million in backlog, excluding additional services and compensation for mobilization and demobilization.

Inclusive of the recently announced fixtures on the Transocean Barents in Norway, and the Deepwater Orion, Deepwater Aquila, and Deepwater Corcovado in Brazil, total backlog additions approximate $1.6 billion since the beginning of April.

A copy of the press release announcing the fixtures referred to above is attached hereto and incorporated herein by reference as Exhibit 99.1.

The full text of this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1451505/000145150526000032/rig-20260416x8k.htm

Any exhibits and associated documents for this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1451505/000145150526000032/0001451505-26-000032-index.htm

Public companies must file a Form 8-K, or current report, with the SEC generally within four days of any event that could materially affect a company's financial position or the value of its shares.

data.title: Gucci-owner’s new strategy looks half dressed
data.description:
By Yawen Chen

France's $40 bln Kering vowed to more than double its operating margin at its capital markets day, but disappointed investors as the target was priced in. As even luxury titan Hermès loses its shine, new CEO Luca de Meo needs to be bolder and copy US brand Coach's success.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow Yawen Chen on Bluesky and LinkedIn.

CONTEXT NEWS

French luxury group Kering said on April 16 that it aims to more than double its operating profit margin in the medium term, as the group laid out a strategy to restore financial health and boost the appeal of flagship brand Gucci.

A statement from Kering said Gucci would improve its product quality and sharpen its regional sales strategy, without giving financial targets for the brand.

Shares in Paris-listed Kering were down 1.5% by 0815 GMT on April 16.
